ClinicalDocument.title: Continuity of Care Document - Henry Martinez

value.displayName: for persistent vomiting to enable oral rehydration

act.text: Well-child examination - 2 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"Henry woke up yesterday morning with a runny nose and has been pretty fussy ever since. This morning he felt warm, so we took his temperature and it was 100.7. He's been sneezing a lot and his nose won't stop running—it's mostly clear. We gave him some children's ibuprofen and tried to get him to drink more, but he's not really interested in eating or drinking like normal. We're mainly worried because he's never had a fever before and we want to make sure it's not something serious."

Upper Respiratory Infection: History and exam consistent with viral URI: fever, rhinorrhea, and mild erythema of nasal mucosa/oropharynx without exudate or consolidation on lung exam. Tachycardia and tachypnea are expected physiologic responses to fever and viral infection in this age group.

- [medication] Acetaminophen 190mg Q4-6H PRN PRN - [medication] Ibuprofen 130mg Q6-8H PRN PRN - [education] Supportive care with rest and increased fluids - [education] Saline nasal drops and bulb suction for congestion - [education] Honey (if >1 year) for cough relief - [education] Return if: difficulty breathing, fever >3 days, ear pain, or worsening symptoms

"He woke up yesterday morning with a fever—it was 103 when I took his temperature. He's been pretty fussy and clingy all day, won't eat much, just wants his bottle and to be held. I gave him some Tylenol this morning and it came down a bit, but it keeps coming back. He's not acting like himself at all, and I'm worried it might be something serious."

Fever: Clinical presentation of fever with tachycardia (HR 114) and otherwise benign exam (normal HEENT, clear lungs bilaterally) is consistent with simple fever, likely viral etiology. Absence of focal findings on exam supports non-emergent fever workup pending clinical course and additional hx.
- [medication] Acetaminophen or ibuprofen for temperature control - [education] Monitor for source of infection, return if fever persists >3 days - [follow-up] Follow up as needed if symptoms worsen
Well-child examination - 2 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

Mom reports Henry developed a red, bumpy rash on his trunk and face starting yesterday morning. She says it started as just a few spots but has spread over the past 24 hours, and he's been fussier than usual and running a fever—she took his temperature yesterday and it was 101.5°F. She's given him ibuprofen a couple times which helps a little, but the rash keeps getting worse. She's mainly worried it might be something serious and wants to make sure it's nothing that needs antibiotics.

Rash: Clinical presentation of rash in toddler w/ mild tachycardia (HR 102) and tachypnea (RR 26) suggests possible viral exanthem or dermatologic process; normal HEENT and clear lungs B/L help exclude serious systemic illness or respiratory compromise, supporting benign rash etiology.
- [other] Topical care as appropriate for rash type - [education] Return if rash spreads, becomes painful, or child develops fever

Henry started running a fever yesterday morning—we took his temperature and it was over 101. Since then he's had a runny nose with clear drainage, and he's been pretty fussy and clingy. He's not eating his usual meals, though he'll drink some juice. We gave him some children's acetaminophen last night which helped a bit, but the fever keeps coming back. We're mainly worried because he seems uncomfortable and we want to make sure it's nothing serious.

Upper Respiratory Infection: Clinical presentation consistent with URI: fever, rhinorrhea, and erythematous nasal mucosa with clear lungs on exam r/o lower respiratory involvement. Tachycardia and tachypnea likely secondary to fever and viral illness rather than respiratory compromise given clear bilateral breath sounds.

- [medication] Acetaminophen 170mg Q4-6H PRN PRN - [medication] Ibuprofen 120mg Q6-8H PRN PRN - [education] Supportive care with rest and increased fluids - [education] Saline nasal drops and bulb suction for congestion - [education] Honey (if >1 year) for cough relief - [education] Return if: difficulty breathing, fever >3 days, ear pain, or worsening symptoms

"He's had a runny nose for about 2 days now, and yesterday he started running a fever. This morning it was 100.8. He's been pretty fussy and not eating as much as usual, mostly just picking at his food and wanting his bottle. We gave him some acetaminophen yesterday which helped a little, but he's still pretty congested. I'm mainly worried because he's not himself—just wants to be held and isn't playing like normal."

Upper Respiratory Infection: Clinical presentation consistent with viral URI: fever, rhinorrhea, and erythematous nasal mucosa/oropharynx with tachycardia and tachypnea (expected physiologic response to fever/viral illness) and clear lungs (no lower respiratory involvement).

- [medication] Acetaminophen 170mg Q4-6H PRN PRN - [medication] Ibuprofen 120mg Q6-8H PRN PRN - [education] Supportive care with rest and increased fluids - [education] Saline nasal drops and bulb suction for congestion - [education] Honey (if >1 year) for cough relief - [education] Return if: difficulty breathing, fever >3 days, ear pain, or worsening symptoms
Well-child examination - 1 year 6 month old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit
Well-child examination - 1 year 3 month old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"He woke up hot yesterday morning around 6 AM and we took his temperature—it was 102.5. He's been really fussy and not interested in his usual breakfast or snacks, mostly just wanting his bottle. We gave him some infant Tylenol yesterday afternoon and it came down a bit, but this morning it was back up to 103. He's also had some loose stools since yesterday but no vomiting. We're worried because he's usually such a happy kid and he's just not himself."

Fever: Clinical presentation consistent w/ fever: Vital signs show tachycardia (HR 125) and tachypnea (RR 26) appropriate for febrile state in 1 yo; however, PE otherwise unremarkable w/ clear lungs and normal HEENT exam, suggesting uncomplicated viral etiology pending further workup.
- [medication] Acetaminophen or ibuprofen for temperature control - [education] Monitor for source of infection, return if fever persists >3 days - [follow-up] Follow up as needed if symptoms worsen
Well-child examination - 1 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit
Well-child examination - 9 month old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

Parent Narrative: "He woke up yesterday morning with a really hoarse, raspy cry—it doesn't sound like his normal cry at all. Last night he had a runny nose and seemed a little congested, but this morning the cough started and it sounds like a seal barking. He's been fussy and we tried running the humidifier and gave him some cool air from the bathroom, but he still sounds really rough. We're worried because he's never sounded like this before and we want to make sure he can breathe okay." --- *This narrative includes:* - Timeline (yesterday morning/last night) - Key observations (hoarse cry, barking cough, congestion progression) - Home remedies attempted (humidifier, cool air) - Parent concern (breathing/severity)

Croup: Clinical presentation consistent w/ croup: inspiratory stridor + hoarse cry + tachypnea (RR 35) + tachycardia (HR 124) in 7mo infant. Clear lung fields exclude pneumonia/foreign body; findings classic for laryngotracheobronchitis, likely viral etiology.

- [medication] Dexamethasone 5mg x1 dose x 1 days - [education] Single dose of dexamethasone is typically curative - [education] Cool mist or night air may provide temporary relief - [education] Keep child calm - crying worsens stridor - [education] Return IMMEDIATELY for stridor at rest, drooling, or respiratory distress

"He started running a fever yesterday afternoon, around 101 degrees. This morning he's been really fussy and won't take his bottles like he normally does—just picks at them. He's had some loose stools too, maybe three or four times since yesterday. We've been giving him cool baths and infant Tylenol, but he just seems uncomfortable and we wanted to make sure it's nothing serious."

Viral Syndrome: Clinical presentation consistent with viral syndrome: tachycardia (HR 133) and tachypnea (RR 32) suggest systemic viral infection with compensatory response, while clear lungs and benign HEENT exam exclude bacterial pneumonia or localized infection.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days
Well-child examination - 6 month old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"He started running a fever yesterday afternoon—we took his temperature and it was 101.5. Today he's been really fussy and won't take his bottle like normal, just picks at it. He's had some loose stools too. We gave him some infant acetaminophen last night and that helped a little, but he's still not himself. We're mostly worried because he's so young and we want to make sure this isn't something serious."

Viral Syndrome: Clinical presentation consistent with viral syndrome: tachycardia (HR 141) and tachypnea (RR 27) suggest systemic viral infection with appropriate physiologic response; however, clear lungs and benign HEENT exam exclude focal bacterial infection or lower respiratory involvement at this time.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days

Henry started running a fever yesterday afternoon—his forehead felt warm so we took his temperature and it was 101.2°F. He's been pretty fussy since then and won't take his bottle like he normally does, only taking small amounts. This morning he developed a runny nose and a little cough. We've been giving him acetaminophen for the fever and it comes down for a few hours, but then he gets hot again. We're mostly worried because he's not eating well and we want to make sure this doesn't turn into something more serious.

Viral Syndrome: Clinical presentation consistent with viral syndrome: tachycardia (HR 139) and tachypnea (RR 38) reflect systemic viral illness, while clear lungs bilaterally exclude lower respiratory tract involvement. Normal HEENT exam and absence of focal findings support uncomplicated viral etiology.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days

"He started having diarrhea yesterday morning—it was greenish and watery, way more than usual. Then last night he threw up twice after his bottle and has been really fussy ever since. He's not interested in eating much today and feels warm to me, so I'm worried he might have a fever. We've been trying to give him small amounts of formula but he won't take much."

Viral Gastroenteritis: Clinical presentation consistent w/ viral gastroenteritis: tachycardia (HR 150) and tachypnea (RR 33) suggest compensatory response to likely hypovolemia from GI losses, while normal HEENT and clear lungs exclude alternative diagnoses (meningitis, pneumonia, sepsis).

- [medication] Ondansetron 1mg x1 dose PRN PRN - [education] Oral rehydration with small frequent amounts (1-2 tsp every 5 minutes) - [education] Use Pedialyte or similar oral rehydration solution - [education] Advance diet as tolerated - no need for BRAT restriction - [education] Return if: unable to keep fluids down, no urine in 8 hours, blood in stool, or appears very ill
Well-child examination - 4 month old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit
Well-child examination - 2 month old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit
Well-child examination - newborn
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit
Healthy newborn
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

tr.td: No developmental concerns at this time
Concerns: poor hand-eye coordination; Discussed concerns with family. Will monitor at next visit.
No developmental concerns at this time
Concerns: not pointing; Discussed concerns with family. Will monitor at next visit.
No developmental concerns at this time
No developmental concerns at this time
